Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: First, compute the total duration for each case from the first event (Submit Claim) to the last event (Close Claim). Well convert timestamps to datetime objects and compute differences. Lets list the events for each case: Case 2001: - Submit: 2024-04-01 09:00 ... Total duration: from 09:00 to 10:30 on same day = 1.5 hours. Case 2002: ... total = 25h55m ... Case 2003: ... total = 48h20m. Case 2004: ... = 1h25m. Case 2005: ... total = 77h5m.

Name: Inductive Reasoning - PE
Text: So durations: 2001:1.5h 2002:25.92h 2003:48.33h 2004:1.42h 2005:77.08h Clearly, cases 2003 and 2005 are much longer. Case 2002 is also longer than 2001 and 2004 but not as extreme.

Name: Pattern Recognition - PE
Text: check complexity: 2001: Low -> short 2002: Medium -> medium? 2003: High -> very long 2004: Low -> short 2005: High -> very long So high complexity seems to correlate with long durations.

Name: Abductive Reasoning - IND
Text: But also region? 2001 and 2003 are in Region A, 2002,2004,2005 in Region B. But 2001 is short, 2003 is long, so region alone not cause. Resource? Not clear.

Name: Backtracking - PE
Text: But we need to analyze attributes. Lets compute durations more systematically and then see correlations. Well compute total time in hours for easier comparison. Case 2001: 1.5 h Case 2002: 25.9167h Case 2003: 48.3333h Case 2004: 1.4167h Case 2005: 77.0833h.

Name: Pattern Recognition - PE
Text: Now, we see that cases with high complexity (2003 and 2005) have very long durations. Case 2002 has medium complexity and duration is also long (25.92h) compared to low complexity cases (1.5h). So complexity seems a factor. But also note that within high complexity, there are multiple requests for additional documents.

Name: Inductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Lets group by complexity: Low: 2001 and 2004. Average duration: (1.5+1.42)/2 = 1.46h. Medium: 2002. Duration: 25.92h. High: 2003 and 2005. Average: (48.33+77.08)/2 = 62.7h. So clearly, high complexity leads to much longer durations. Now by region: Region A: average 24.9h. Region B: average 34.47h. But note that region A has only two cases, one low and one high. So the average is skewed.

Name: Pattern Recognition - PE
Text: Lets count the number of Request Additional Documents events per case: 2001: 0 2002: 1 2003: 2 2004: 0 2005: 3 So the number of requests correlates with duration: 0 -> short, 1 -> medium, 2 -> long, 3 -> very long.

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: Thus, the root cause is the complexity level of the claim, which leads to multiple rounds of requests for additional documents, which extend the process. Also, even medium complexity requires one round, which adds time.

Name: Validation - PE
Text: For high complexity, the time between the last request and approval is still long (about 22 hours). That might be because the manager needs to review... But the main issue is the multiple requests.
